---
title: "Higher-Order Network Analysis"
subtitle: "Sequential Memory, Path Anomalies, and Topological Structure in Behavioral Data"
author:
  - name: Mohammed Saqr
    url: https://saqr.me
    affiliation: University of Eastern Finland
  - name: Sonsoles López-Pernas
    url: https://sonsoles.me
    affiliation: University of Eastern Finland
  - name: Kamila Misiejuk
    url: https://kamilamisiejuk.com
    affiliation: FernUniversität in Hagen, Germany
date: "2026-04-15"
categories:
  - tutorial
  - R
  - higher-order networks
  - Nestimate
warning: false
message: false
fig.align: center
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
    theme: cosmo
    self-contained: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    fig.width: 10
    fig.height: 7
    dpi: 300
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(Nestimate)
library(cograph)
```

# Introduction

Network representations of sequential data have become a central tool in the behavioral sciences. Transition Network Analysis (TNA), which models behavior as a directed graph of state-to-state transition probabilities, has been widely adopted in learning analytics (Saqr et al., 2025), process mining, and interaction analysis. The standard TNA framework rests on a first-order Markov assumption: the probability of transitioning to the next state depends only on the current state, with no memory of the path that led there.

This assumption is often violated. Human behavior exhibits sequential dependencies that extend beyond the immediately preceding state. A student who reaches a specification phase after a critique behaves differently than one who arrives at the same state after a direct instruction --- even though the current state is identical. These **higher-order dependencies** have been documented in navigation patterns on the web (Scholtes, 2017), mobility trajectories (Xu et al., 2016), learning processes (Saqr & López-Pernas, 2025), and communication networks (Lambiotte et al., 2019). Ignoring them leads to models that underfit the data and obscure genuinely important sequential structure.

Detecting and characterizing higher-order dependencies requires a different class of methods than standard network analysis. Where a first-order model asks "what is the probability of transitioning from state A to state B?", higher-order methods ask a richer set of questions: Does sequential memory exist in this data, and at what depth? Which specific states are context-dependent? Which multi-step sequences occur far more or less often than expected? What is the topological shape of the higher-order interaction structure?

This chapter provides a comprehensive treatment of higher-order network analysis as implemented in the Nestimate and cograph R packages. We cover five families of methods:

1. **Multi-Order Model Selection (MOGen)** --- statistical tests for whether higher-order dependencies exist and at what order.
2. **Higher-Order Networks (HON)** --- state-space expansion that identifies where context changes transitions.
3. **Path Anomaly Detection (HYPA)** --- hypothesis testing for individual pathway frequencies against a null model.
4. **Simplicial Complex Analysis** --- topological invariants (Betti numbers, Euler characteristic) and their persistence across scales.
5. **Statistical Group Comparison** --- permutation testing and sequence pattern comparison across conditions.

Each section combines theoretical background with practical implementation, demonstrating the full range of parameters available in each function. Visualizations appear alongside each method rather than in a separate section, so that the visual output is always immediately available for interpretation.

# Data and first-order network

We use a dataset of student-AI interactions from a prompt engineering course. Students composed prompts for an AI coding assistant across multiple assignments, with each action coded into one of eight categories: Agreement, Context, Critique, Instruct, Positive emotion, Reasoning, Refine, and Specification. The `submission_id` column identifies individual submission sequences, and the `Timing` column distinguishes Early from Late phases of the course.

```{r}
#| label: data
csv_path <- system.file("extdata", "prompting_data.csv",
                        package = "Nestimate")
prompts <- read.csv(csv_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

We begin with a standard first-order transition network. `build_network()` accepts long-format data directly, handling format conversion, session detection, and matrix computation internally:

```{r}
#| label: build-network
net <- build_network(prompts, method = "relative",
                     action = "Code", actor = "submission_id")
net
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-base
#| fig-cap: "First-order transition network of AI prompting strategies. Edge widths and opacity reflect transition probabilities; edges below 0.03 are hidden for clarity."
splot(net, minimum = 0.03, title = "AI Prompting Strategies")
```

The network reveals a dominant *Instruct -> Specification* loop and a secondary *Refine -> Reasoning* cycle. But this representation treats all arrivals at a given state identically --- it cannot distinguish *Critique -> Specification* from *Instruct -> Specification*, even though the behavioral context differs substantially. The methods that follow address exactly this limitation.

# Multi-order model selection (MOGen)

## Theoretical background

The Multi-Order Generative Model (Scholtes, 2017) provides a principled statistical framework for determining whether higher-order dependencies exist in sequential data and, if so, at what order they operate. The approach fits a family of Markov chain models at increasing orders $k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$ and selects the optimal order using information-theoretic criteria.

At order $k$, the model conditions transition probabilities on the preceding $k$ states rather than just the current state. For $k = 1$, this is the standard first-order Markov chain. For $k = 2$, the probability of transitioning to state $s_{t+1}$ depends on the pair $(s_{t-1}, s_t)$. The log-likelihood increases with $k$ (a higher-order model can always fit the data at least as well as a lower-order one), but so does the number of parameters. Model selection balances fit against complexity.

Scholtes (2017) proposed using both AIC and BIC for this comparison. AIC penalizes each additional parameter by 2, while BIC penalizes by $\log(n)$, making BIC more conservative for large samples. A likelihood ratio test (LRT) provides a complementary frequentist approach: it tests whether the improvement in fit from order $k$ to $k+1$ is statistically significant.

The practical importance of this step cannot be overstated. If the optimal order is 1, the standard TNA model is adequate and higher-order methods are unnecessary. If the optimal order is greater than 1, the data contains genuine sequential memory that first-order models systematically miss. Even when the globally optimal order is 1, individual pathways may still exhibit higher-order behavior --- a situation that HON and HYPA are designed to detect.

## Implementation

`build_mogen()` fits Markov models from order 1 to `max_order` and returns AIC, BIC, log-likelihood, and degrees of freedom at each order. The `criterion` argument controls which criterion is used for optimal order selection (`"aic"`, `"bic"`, or `"lrt"`).

```{r}
#| label: mogen
mg <- build_mogen(net, max_order = 4, criterion = "bic")
summary(mg)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-mogen
#| fig-cap: "AIC and BIC curves across Markov orders. The arrow marks the optimal order for each criterion. A decreasing curve indicates that additional memory improves model fit even after penalizing for complexity."
#| fig-height: 5
plot(mg)
```

## Extracting transition tables

`mogen_transitions()` extracts the transition table at any fitted order. Each row represents a transition from a source context to a target state, with the observed count and estimated probability. The `min_count` argument filters rare transitions.

```{r}
#| label: mogen-trans-2
mt2 <- mogen_transitions(mg, order = 2)
head(mt2, 8)
```

At order 3, the source contexts are three states long, revealing deeper sequential patterns:

```{r}
#| label: mogen-trans-3
mt3 <- mogen_transitions(mg, order = 3, min_count = 10)
head(mt3, 8)
```

These tables make it possible to compare, for example, how the probability of reaching *Specification* changes depending on whether the preceding context was *Instruct -> Instruct* (systematic repetition) versus *Critique -> Reasoning* (reflective correction).

# Higher-order networks (HON)

## Theoretical background

The Higher-Order Network framework (Xu et al., 2016) takes a different approach to detecting sequential memory. Rather than fitting a global model at a single order, HON tests each state individually: does adding one more step of context significantly change the outgoing transition distribution for this particular state?

The algorithm proceeds iteratively. Starting from the first-order network, HON considers each state $s$ and tests whether splitting $s$ into context-dependent variants (e.g., splitting *Specification* into *Instruct -> Specification* and *Critique -> Specification*) produces statistically different outgoing transition distributions. If the distributions differ significantly (measured by the Kullback-Leibler divergence, thresholded by a minimum frequency), the split is retained and the process continues to the next order.

This produces a **variable-order** network: some states remain first-order (their context does not matter), while others are expanded to second, third, or higher orders. The result is a heterogeneous network where each node represents a state with a specific history, and edges capture context-dependent transitions. Xu et al. (2016) showed that this representation can dramatically improve the accuracy of network-based predictions in a variety of domains, from web navigation to flight itineraries.

The key advantage of HON over a global higher-order model is parsimony: it only expands states where context actually matters, avoiding the combinatorial explosion of parameters that a full $k$-th order model requires.

## Implementation

`build_hon()` constructs the higher-order network. The key parameters are:

- `max_order`: maximum order to consider (default 5).
- `min_freq`: minimum number of observations for a context to be considered for splitting. Higher values produce sparser, more conservative networks.
- `collapse_repeats`: whether to merge consecutive identical states before analysis.
- `method`: `"hon+"` (default) uses the enhanced splitting criterion.

```{r}
#| label: hon
hon <- build_hon(net, max_order = 5, min_freq = 1)
hon
```

Starting from `r length(hon$first_order_states)` original states, HON expands the network to `r hon$n_nodes` context-dependent nodes, with dependencies observed up to order `r hon$max_order_observed`. The arrow notation in node labels reads as a history: `"Context -> Instruct -> Specification"` means *Specification, preceded by Instruct, preceded by Context*.

## Extracting and filtering pathways

The `pathways()` function extracts higher-order pathways from HON results. It provides several filtering options:

- `top`: maximum number of pathways to return.
- `min_count`: minimum transition count (filters noise from rare observations).
- `min_prob`: minimum transition probability (filters weak transitions).
- `order`: restrict to a specific order level.

```{r}
#| label: pathways-hon
pathways(hon, top = 8)
```

```{r}
#| label: pathways-hon-filtered
# Second-order pathways with at least 5 observations
pathways(hon, min_count = 5, order = 2, top = 5)
```

`pathways()` also works directly on any `netobject`, building the HON internally:

```{r}
#| label: pathways-from-net
pathways(net, ho_method = "hon", top = 5)
```

## Visualizing HON pathways

cograph's `plot_simplicial()` renders higher-order pathways as colored blob diagrams overlaid on the network layout. Each blob traces a pathway through the network: source nodes appear in blue, the target node in orange. The function accepts `netobject`, `net_hon`, and `net_hypa` objects directly.

```{r}
#| label: fig-hon-combined
#| fig-cap: "Top 6 HON pathways overlaid on the network. Overlapping blobs indicate states that participate in multiple higher-order patterns."
plot_simplicial(net, method = "hon", max_pathways = 6,
                title = "Top Higher-Order Pathways")
```

The `dismantled` parameter renders each pathway in its own panel:

```{r}
#| label: fig-hon-dismantled
#| fig-cap: "Dismantled view --- each panel isolates one higher-order pathway for easier interpretation."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 10
plot_simplicial(hon, max_pathways = 6,
                dismantled = TRUE, ncol = 3)
```

## Raw path frequencies

`path_counts()` counts the most frequent $k$-step sequences in the raw data, regardless of whether they exhibit statistically significant higher-order behavior. This provides a descriptive baseline:

```{r}
#| label: path-counts
path_counts(net, k = 3, top = 10)
```

# Path anomaly detection (HYPA)

## Theoretical background

While MOGen tests whether higher-order dependencies exist globally and HON identifies where context changes transitions locally, **HYPA** (Hypergeometric Path Anomaly detection; LaRock et al., 2020) asks a more targeted question: which specific multi-step sequences occur significantly more or less often than expected?

The null model is elegant. HYPA preserves the first-order transition structure exactly --- the same in-degrees, out-degrees, and edge weights as the observed network --- and asks what path frequencies we would expect if transitions were generated by this first-order process. Deviations from this expectation indicate genuine higher-order effects: sequences that are reinforced or suppressed beyond what first-order dynamics predict.

Formally, for a path $p = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k)$, HYPA computes the expected count under a **multi-hypergeometric null model**. The observed count is then compared to this expectation using the hypergeometric cumulative distribution function. Paths with significantly more observations than expected are classified as **over-represented** (behavioral routines, habitual sequences), while those with significantly fewer are **under-represented** (avoided combinations, suppressed transitions).

The statistical framework has several attractive properties. It is non-parametric (no distributional assumptions beyond the null model). It naturally handles the discrete, count-based nature of path data. And because it tests individual paths rather than global model fit, it can detect localized higher-order structure even when the globally optimal Markov order is 1.

Over-represented paths are particularly interesting in behavioral research. They represent learned sequences that participants perform far more often than random first-order transitions would predict --- behavioral routines, cognitive strategies, or habitual interaction patterns. Under-represented paths, conversely, reveal systematically avoided combinations that may reflect cognitive constraints, social norms, or task structure.

## Implementation

`build_hypa()` computes path anomaly scores. The key parameters are:

- `k`: path length (default 3, meaning 4-state paths including the starting state).
- `alpha`: significance level for anomaly classification (default 0.05).
- `min_count`: minimum observed count for a path to be tested (default 5).
- `p_adjust`: multiple testing correction method (default `"BH"` for Benjamini-Hochberg).

```{r}
#| label: hypa
hypa <- build_hypa(net, k = 3, alpha = 0.05, min_count = 5, p_adjust = "BH")
hypa
```

## Inspecting results

`summary()` displays the top anomalous paths. The `type` argument focuses on `"over"`, `"under"`, or `"all"` anomalies, and `n` controls how many to show:

```{r}
#| label: hypa-over
summary(hypa, type = "over", n = 8)
```

The **ratio** column is the key interpretive quantity: a ratio of 15 means the path occurs 15 times more often than the first-order model predicts.

```{r}
#| label: hypa-under
summary(hypa, type = "under", n = 8)
```

Ratios below 0.5 indicate paths occurring less than half as often as expected.

## Visualizing HYPA results

`plot_simplicial()` accepts HYPA objects directly. The `anomaly` argument selects which pathways to display:

```{r}
#| label: fig-hypa-over
#| fig-cap: "Over-represented pathways --- behavioral routines students repeat far more often than expected under the first-order model."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 10
plot_simplicial(hypa, anomaly = "over", max_pathways = 6,
                dismantled = TRUE, ncol = 3)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-hypa-under
#| fig-cap: "Under-represented pathways --- sequences students systematically avoid."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 7
plot_simplicial(hypa, anomaly = "under", max_pathways = 6,
                dismantled = TRUE, ncol = 3)
```

## Pathway extraction

`pathways()` on a HYPA object extracts pathway strings for downstream analysis, with `type` to filter:

```{r}
#| label: pathways-hypa
pathways(hypa, type = "over")[1:5]
```

## Customizing pathway plots

`plot_simplicial()` provides extensive control over the visual appearance:

- `node_color` / `target_color`: source and target node colors.
- `blob_alpha`: transparency of blob fills (default 0.25).
- `blob_colors`: individual blob fill colors (recycled).
- `shadow`: soft drop shadows (default TRUE).
- `node_size` / `label_size`: point and text sizing.
- `layout`: `"circle"` (default) or a coordinate matrix.

```{r}
#| label: fig-custom
#| fig-cap: "Customized pathway visualization with modified colors and reduced transparency."
plot_simplicial(net, max_pathways = 4,
                node_color = "#2C3E50", target_color = "#E74C3C",
                blob_alpha = 0.15, node_size = 25, label_size = 4,
                title = "Custom Styled Pathways")
```

# Statistical group comparison

## Permutation testing

A central question in applied research is whether the network structure differs between groups. Nestimate's `permutation()` provides a non-parametric test for edge-level differences. It shuffles the group labels across the combined dataset, re-estimates both networks at each iteration, and compares the observed edge differences to the permutation null distribution. The result identifies which specific edges are significantly stronger in one group than the other.

```{r}
#| label: group-networks
grp_net <- build_network(prompts, method = "relative",
                         action = "Code", actor = "submission_id",
                         group = "Timing")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-group-nets
#| fig-cap: "First-order transition networks by course phase (common weight scale)."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6
splot(grp_net, minimum = 0.03)
```

```{r}
#| label: perm-test
#| cache: true
set.seed(42)
perm <- permutation(grp_net$Early, grp_net$Late, iter = 1000)
perm
```

`splot()` renders the permutation result as a coloured difference network --- green edges are significantly stronger in the first group (Early), red edges in the second (Late):

```{r}
#| label: fig-perm
#| fig-cap: "Permutation test: green edges are significantly stronger in Early, red in Late (p < 0.05). Dashed grey edges are non-significant."
splot(perm, show_nonsig = TRUE, show_stars = TRUE)
```

## Sequence pattern comparison

While permutation testing compares edge-level network structure, `sequence_compare()` compares the frequency of **multi-step sequential patterns** (k-grams) between groups. It extracts all k-grams of specified lengths, counts them per group, and uses a permutation test to identify patterns whose frequency differs significantly between conditions.

This is particularly relevant for higher-order analysis: it tests whether the sequential *patterns* differ between groups, not just the pairwise *transitions*.

```{r}
#| label: seq-compare
#| cache: true
set.seed(42)
sc <- sequence_compare(grp_net, sub = 2:4, iter = 1000)
sc
```

`sequence_compare()` returns a `net_sequence_comparison` object with a tidy data frame of patterns, their per-group frequencies, Pearson residuals, effect sizes, and permutation p-values. The `plot()` method renders a back-to-back residual pyramid:

```{r}
#| label: fig-seq-compare
#| fig-cap: "Sequence pattern comparison between Early and Late phases. Bars show Pearson residuals; longer bars indicate patterns that differ more from independence. Stars indicate statistical significance."
plot(sc)
```

## Comparing higher-order structure with HYPA

Permutation testing and sequence comparison operate at the first-order level --- they compare pairwise transitions or k-gram frequencies. A deeper question is whether the **higher-order** structure itself differs between groups: do the same paths deviate from first-order expectations in both conditions, or do new behavioral routines emerge (or disappear) over time?

HYPA provides a natural framework for this comparison. Because HYPA tests each path individually against a hypergeometric null model (LaRock et al., 2020), running it on each group separately yields per-path p-values in each condition. A path that is significantly over-represented in one group but normal in another represents a genuine group-level difference in higher-order structure --- a behavioral routine that is present in one condition but absent in the other. The null model is the group's own first-order structure, so deviations are relative to each group's baseline rather than to some global reference.

```{r}
#| label: group-hypa
h_early <- build_hypa(grp_net$Early)
h_late <- build_hypa(grp_net$Late)

# Summary counts
data.frame(
  group = c("Early", "Late"),
  total_paths = c(h_early$n_edges, h_late$n_edges),
  anomalous = c(h_early$n_anomalous, h_late$n_anomalous),
  over = c(h_early$n_over, h_late$n_over),
  under = c(h_early$n_under, h_late$n_under),
  pct_anomalous = round(100 * c(h_early$n_anomalous / h_early$n_edges,
                                 h_late$n_anomalous / h_late$n_edges), 1)
)
```

The group with a higher percentage of anomalous pathways exhibits more higher-order structure --- its behavioral sequences deviate more from first-order predictions.

### Identifying group-specific routines

The most informative comparison identifies paths whose anomaly status *differs* between groups. A path over-represented in Late but normal in Early is a behavioral routine that emerged over time; one over-represented in Early but normal in Late is a routine that was abandoned.

```{r}
#| label: group-hypa-diff
# Collect all paths that are anomalous in at least one group
all_anom <- union(
  union(h_early$over$path, h_early$under$path),
  union(h_late$over$path, h_late$under$path)
)

# Merge HYPA scores for these paths
e_scores <- h_early$scores[h_early$scores$path %in% all_anom,
                            c("path", "ratio", "anomaly")]
l_scores <- h_late$scores[h_late$scores$path %in% all_anom,
                           c("path", "ratio", "anomaly")]
comp <- merge(e_scores, l_scores, by = "path",
              suffixes = c("_early", "_late"), all = TRUE)
comp <- comp[order(-abs(comp$ratio_late - comp$ratio_early)), ]
head(comp, 12)
```

Paths where the anomaly column differs between groups (e.g., `"normal"` in Early but `"over"` in Late) represent statistically supported group differences in higher-order structure --- each side is backed by its own hypergeometric p-value.

### Visualizing group-specific pathways

```{r}
#| label: fig-group-simplicial
#| fig-cap: "Higher-order pathways by course phase. Differences in blob patterns reveal which sequential routines emerge or disappear over time."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 7
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot_simplicial(grp_net$Early, method = "hypa", max_pathways = 4,
                title = "Early Phase")
plot_simplicial(grp_net$Late, method = "hypa", max_pathways = 4,
                title = "Late Phase")
```

# Simplicial complex analysis

## Theoretical background

The methods above analyze sequential pathways --- ordered sequences of states over time. **Simplicial complex analysis** addresses a complementary question: what is the topological structure of the network itself?

A simplicial complex is a mathematical generalization of a network that captures group interactions beyond pairwise connections. In a standard graph, the basic unit of interaction is an edge connecting two nodes. In a simplicial complex, the basic unit is a **simplex**: a fully connected subgraph of any size. A 0-simplex is a node, a 1-simplex is an edge, a 2-simplex is a filled triangle (three mutually connected nodes), a 3-simplex is a tetrahedron (four mutually connected nodes), and so on.

The motivation for this generalization comes from the observation that many real-world interactions are inherently multi-body. In a behavioral network, three states that are all mutually connected form a tighter cluster than three states connected by only two edges. The simplicial representation preserves this distinction, which ordinary graph-theoretic measures (degree, betweenness, clustering coefficient) do not.

Topological data analysis (TDA) provides a suite of invariants for characterizing simplicial complexes (Edelsbrunner & Harer, 2008; Otter et al., 2017). The most important are:

- **Betti numbers** ($\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots$): $\beta_0$ counts connected components, $\beta_1$ counts independent loops, $\beta_2$ counts enclosed voids.
- **Euler characteristic** ($\chi = \sum_{k} (-1)^k f_k$): a single integer summarizing the alternating sum of simplex counts.
- **The f-vector** $(f_0, f_1, f_2, \ldots)$: the count of simplices at each dimension.

## Building the simplicial complex

`build_simplicial()` constructs a simplicial complex from a network or from higher-order pathways. The `type` argument selects the construction method:

- `"clique"`: extracts all maximal cliques from the thresholded network.
- `"pathway"`: treats each higher-order pathway as a simplex.

A metric Vietoris-Rips filtration is not implemented; `type = "vr"` raises an error rather than silently returning a clique complex.

### Clique complex

```{r}
#| label: sc-clique
sc <- build_simplicial(net, type = "clique", threshold = 0.05)
sc
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-sc-clique
#| fig-cap: "Simplicial complex diagnostics: f-vector, Betti numbers, simplicial degree distribution, and adjacency heatmap."
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 9
plot(sc)
```

### Pathway complex

Building a simplicial complex from HON pathways reveals the topology of sequential patterns:

```{r}
#| label: sc-pathway
sc_pathway <- build_simplicial(hon, type = "pathway", max_pathways = 30)
sc_pathway
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-sc-pathway
#| fig-cap: "Simplicial complex built from HON pathways --- topology of sequential patterns."
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 9
plot(sc_pathway)
```

## Topological descriptors

```{r}
#| label: betti
betti_numbers(sc)
euler_characteristic(sc)
```

$\beta_0 = 1$ indicates a single connected component. The Euler characteristic of 1 confirms the complex is contractible.

### Simplicial degree

`simplicial_degree()` measures how many higher-dimensional simplices each node participates in:

```{r}
#| label: simplicial-degree
simplicial_degree(sc)
```

### Verification

`verify_simplicial()` independently verifies the clique decomposition and the Euler-Poincare relation:

```{r}
#| label: verify
verify_simplicial(net$weights, threshold = 0.05)
```

## Persistent homology

A single simplicial complex at a fixed threshold provides a snapshot of topological structure. But the choice of threshold is arbitrary. **Persistent homology** (Edelsbrunner & Harer, 2008) resolves this by tracking how topological features appear and disappear as the threshold varies continuously.

The key idea is a **filtration**: a nested sequence of simplicial complexes obtained by gradually lowering the threshold. Each topological feature has a **birth** (the threshold at which it appears) and a **death** (the threshold at which it disappears). Features that persist over a wide range are topologically significant; short-lived features are noise.

```{r}
#| label: persistent
ph <- persistent_homology(net, n_steps = 25, max_dim = 3)
ph
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-persistent
#| fig-cap: "Left: Betti curves showing how topological features evolve across thresholds. Right: persistence diagram, where distance from the diagonal indicates feature robustness."
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 6
plot(ph)
```

## Q-analysis

Q-analysis (Atkin, 1974) measures connectivity at multiple levels of structural sharing. At Q-level $q$, two simplices are $q$-connected if they share at least $q+1$ vertices. The structure vector reveals the depth of structural coupling.

```{r}
#| label: q-analysis
qa <- q_analysis(sc)
qa
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-qanalysis
#| fig-cap: "Structure vector from Q-analysis. The rate of decrease indicates the depth of structural coupling in the network."
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 6
plot(qa)
```

# Higher-order network embedding (HONEM)

The Higher-Order Network Embedding (HONEM; Rossi et al., 2018) maps the expanded HON state space into a continuous vector space. Each higher-order node receives a low-dimensional embedding vector based on SVD of the HON's multi-step reachability structure. The `dim` parameter controls dimensionality; `max_power` controls how many steps of reachability are included.

```{r}
#| label: honem
honem <- build_honem(hon, dim = 16, max_power = 10)
honem
```

The embeddings can be used for clustering higher-order states, link prediction, or as features for downstream models.

# Summary

| Method | Function | Question | Key parameters |
|--------|----------|----------|----------------|
| **MOGen** | `build_mogen()` | Does memory matter? At what order? | `max_order`, `criterion` |
| **HON** | `build_hon()` | Where does context change transitions? | `max_order`, `min_freq`, `method` |
| **HYPA** | `build_hypa()` | Which sequences deviate from expectation? | `k`, `alpha`, `min_count`, `p_adjust` |
| **Pathways** | `pathways()` | Extract pathway strings for analysis/plotting | `top`, `min_count`, `type`, `order` |
| **Visualization** | `plot_simplicial()` | Render pathways as blob diagrams | `max_pathways`, `anomaly`, `dismantled` |
| **Permutation** | `permutation()` | Do edges differ between groups? | `iter`, `alpha`, `adjust` |
| **Seq. comparison** | `sequence_compare()` | Do k-gram patterns differ between groups? | `sub`, `iter`, `min_freq` |
| **Simplicial** | `build_simplicial()` | What is the topological shape? | `type`, `threshold`, `max_dim` |
| **Persistence** | `persistent_homology()` | How robust is topology across scales? | `n_steps`, `max_dim` |
| **Q-analysis** | `q_analysis()` | How deep is structural sharing? | (takes a `simplicial_complex`) |
| **HONEM** | `build_honem()` | Embed HON in continuous space | `dim`, `max_power` |

These methods form a coherent pipeline. MOGen answers whether higher-order analysis is warranted. HON and HYPA identify where and what the higher-order patterns are. `plot_simplicial()` renders them visually. Permutation testing and sequence comparison provide statistical tests for group differences at both the edge level and the sequential pattern level. Simplicial analysis, persistent homology, and Q-analysis characterize the topological shape of the underlying structure.

The critical insight is that each method answers a different question, and no single method subsumes the others. A dataset can have a globally optimal first-order model (MOGen selects $k=1$) but still contain strong local higher-order effects (HYPA finds many anomalous paths). A network can have simple topology ($\beta_1 = 0$, no loops) but rich higher-order sequential structure (HON creates many expanded nodes). Using these methods in combination provides a comprehensive view that any single approach would miss.

# References

- Atkin, R. H. (1974). *Mathematical Structure in Human Affairs*. Heinemann Educational.
- Battiston, F., Cencetti, G., Iacopini, I., Latora, V., Lucas, M., Patania, A., ... & Petri, G. (2020). Networks beyond pairwise interactions: Structure and dynamics. *Physics Reports*, 874, 1--92.
- Edelsbrunner, H., & Harer, J. (2008). Persistent homology---a survey. In *Surveys on Discrete and Computational Geometry* (pp. 257--282). AMS.
- Lambiotte, R., Rosvall, M., & Scholtes, I. (2019). From networks to optimal higher-order models of complex systems. *Nature Physics*, 15(4), 313--320.
- LaRock, T., Scholtes, I., & Eliassi-Rad, T. (2020). HYPA: Efficient detection of path anomalies in time series data on networks. *EPJ Data Science*, 9(1), 15.
- Otter, N., Porter, M. A., Tillmann, U., Grindrod, P., & Harrington, H. A. (2017). A roadmap for the computation of persistent homology. *EPJ Data Science*, 6(1), 17.
- Petri, G., Expert, P., Turkheimer, F., Carhart-Harris, R., Nutt, D., Hellyer, P. J., & Vaccarino, F. (2014). Homological scaffolds of brain functional networks. *Journal of the Royal Society Interface*, 11(101), 20140873.
- Rossi, R. A., Ahmed, N. K., & Koh, E. (2018). Higher-order network representation learning. In *Companion Proceedings of the Web Conference 2018* (pp. 3--4).
- Saqr, M., & López-Pernas, S. (2025). Transition network analysis. In M. Saqr & S. López-Pernas (Eds.), *Learning Analytics Methods and Tutorials* (2nd ed.). Springer.
- Saqr, M., López-Pernas, S., Conde, M., & Hernández-García, Á. (2025). Higher-order networks in learning analytics. *Proceedings of LAK25*.
- Scholtes, I. (2017). When is a network a network? Multi-order graphical model selection in pathways and temporal networks. In *KDD* (pp. 1037--1046).
- Xu, J., Wickramarathne, T. L., & Chawla, N. V. (2016). Representing higher-order dependencies in networks. *Science Advances*, 2(5), e1600028.

---

```{r}
#| label: session
sessionInfo()
```
